Article.ArticleTitle: Passive transfer - is it time to reset our goals?

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Farm Animal Health and Production Group, Royal Veterinary College, Hawkshead Lane, North Mymms, Hatfield, Hertfordshire, AL9 7TA.

Reference.Citation: Smith T, Little RB. The significance of colostrum to the new-born calf. J Exp Med 1922;36:181-98
McEwan AD, Fisher EW, Selman IE. Observations on the immune globulin levels of neonatal calves and their relationship to disease. J Comp Pathol 1970;80:259-65
Tyler JW, Hancock DD, Parish SM, et al. Evaluation of 3 assays for failure of passive transfer in calves. J Vet Int Med 1996;10:304-7
Weaver DM, Tyler JW, VanMetre DC, et al. Passive transfer of colostral immunoglobulins in calves. J Vet Int Med 2000;14:569-77
Donovan GA, Dohoo IR, Montgomery DM, et al. Associations between passive immunity and morbidity and mortality in dairy heifers in Florida, USA. Prev Vet Med 1998;34:31-46
Windeyer MC, Leslie KE, Godden SM, et al. Factors associated with morbidity, mortality, and growth of dairy heifer calves up to 3 months of age. Prev Vet Med 2014;113:231-40
Johnson K, Burn C, Wathes C. Rates and risk factors for contagious disease and mortality in young dairy heifers. https://bit.ly/3EzN4eu (accessed 23 February 2023)

Urie NJ, Lombard JE, Shivley CB, et al. Preweaned heifer management on US dairy operations: part V. Factors associated with morbidity and mortality in preweaned dairy heifer calves. J Dairy Sci 2018;101:9229-44
Lombard J, Urie N, Garry F, et al. Consensus recommendations on calf- and herd-level passive immunity in dairy calves in the United States. J Dairy Sci 2020;103:7611-24
